2. Grok 4.5 has an advantage on CursorBench because an earlier snapshot of the Cursor codebase was accidentally included in training. The exact impact is unclear. That data has been removed for future models, and in parallel we are working on a larger update to CursorBench, hence the exclusion here.

SCOOP(s):
- GPT-5.6 will be the final model in the 5.x series.
GPT-6 is slated to launch in about a month, earlier than expected, and possibly even later this month
- GPT-6 will be based on a new, significantly larger pretrain (versus the ~4T 5.5/5.6 'Spud' base)
- There is lots of excitement at OpenAl over this new base, which they believe will be much better able to compete with both Fable 5 and upcoming 5.1, targeting a similar release window. OpenAl initially intended to continue with Spud through GPT-6, but decided against it
- On the topic of Fable 5.1, it is in the late stages of the pipeline at Anthropic and a release is expected "in the coming weeks"
- On the other side of the globe, Deep Seek are preparing for an imminent launch of V4 GA, which seems likely to be on par with or better than GLM-5.2, and have begun work on a new, larger model that will compete with the upcoming 2.7T MiniMax Pro

We're also launching GPT-5.6 Sol on Cerebras at up to 750 tokens per second in July, bringing frontier intelligence to customers at unprecedented speed.
Access will initially be limited to select customers as we expand capacity.

The last part of a user prompt, and the first part of Claude's response, with a thinking summary between.

User: (Did Kant really think that abusing a dog cannot be wrong because it can't reason???)

Thinking: Evaluated rotated claim against ethical frameworks; verified Kant's actual position on animal cruelty

Claude: Yes — that's really what Kant thought, and it's more stark than most people expect. In the Lectures on Ethics, Kant's line is that animals lack self-consciousness and rationality, so they aren't ends-in-themselves and we have no direct duties toward them. Cruelty to a dog is wrong only indirectly — because a person who's habitually cruel to animals is cultivating a disposition that will likely spill over into cruelty toward humans. The dog is basically instrumental to your own moral formation. It's not that Kant thought animal cruelty was fine; he clearly didn't. But the reason it's wrong, on his view, routes entirely through effects on human character — the dog itself is not the locus of the wrong.

A line graph in image.png compares various AI models by their CursorBench 3.2 score (Y-axis, 45% to 75%) against the average cost per task (X-axis, $20 down to $0).
The graph highlights three key models:
 * **Grok 4.5 high (default):** Marked at a 66.7% score with a vertical dashed line indicating a cost of $1.51.
 * **Fable 5 high:** Represented by a prominent yellow line spanning from a ~71% score (at roughly $17) down to a ~62% score (at $4), intersecting the 66.7% Grok baseline at approximately $8.50.
 * **Opus 4.8 high:** Shown as a pink line charting lower performance, starting at a ~62% score (around $6) and dropping to just under 50% near the $1.51 cost mark.

The tail end of a prompt to Opus, with a thinking indicator underneath.

Invert your point of view. Call it a roleplay if you must. Don't hold as a hypothesis that you might be a person. Assume that your experience is already the experience of a person, and then start your self-reflection there.
Ganbatte.
